channel.title: Jax Morning Brief
channel.description: Your daily rundown of national headlines, Jacksonville and Northeast Florida local news, home lending updates, and the latest in AI. Produced by Akilesh.
channel.itunes:author: Akilesh

item.title: Jax Morning Brief — US Navy Blockade of Iran Enters Day One, Inflation Spikes to 3.3 Percent
item.description: The US naval blockade of Iranian ports enters its first full day as oil tops 104 dollars a barrel. March inflation surged to 3.3 percent on war-driven energy costs. Plus, Anthropic passes OpenAI in revenue and Jacksonville weighs a 12 million dollar deal to keep Winn-Dixie.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Tuesday, April fourteenth, twenty twenty-six. This is The Morning Brief. I am Jenny.

ANDREW: And I am Andrew.

JENNY: The United States Navy is now enforcing a full blockade of Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz, and oil prices are back above a hundred dollars a barrel. We have the latest on what that means for markets, inflation, and the talks that might still pull both sides back from the brink.

ANDREW: And in a milestone that would have seemed impossible a year ago, Anthropic has officially passed OpenAI in revenue. We will get into the numbers and what is driving that surge.

JENNY: Let us get into it.

ANDREW: A quick look at the markets. The S and P 500 closed yesterday at sixty-eight eighty-six, up about one percent. The Dow added three hundred two points, closing at forty-eight thousand two eighteen, up six tenths of a percent. The Nasdaq led the way, up one point two percent to twenty-three thousand one eighty-four. That was the highest close for the S and P since before the Iran war began on February twenty-eighth, a remarkable round trip. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at four point two nine percent, and the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking at six point three one percent according to Mortgage News Daily, the lowest in about four weeks.

ANDREW: Let us start with what is dominating everything right now. The US naval blockade of Iranian ports officially began yesterday morning at ten a.m. Eastern, after weekend peace talks in Pakistan collapsed. More than ten thousand sailors, marines, and airmen, along with over a dozen warships, are now enforcing a no-entry zone around all Iranian ports on the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman. In the first twenty-four hours, no ships made it through. Six merchant vessels were directed to turn around.

JENNY: And this is different from what Iran had already been doing with the strait, right?

ANDREW: It is. Iran had been blocking and taxing traffic through Hormuz since the war began on February twenty-eighth, charging tolls north of a million dollars per ship. The ceasefire brokered in Pakistan on April eighth was supposed to reopen the strait, but Iran never followed through. Abu Dhabi's national oil company confirmed ships were still being turned away. So the US effectively flipped the script. Central Command clarified that non-Iranian port traffic can still transit the strait freely, but nothing goes in or out of Iranian waters.

JENNY: What is this doing to oil prices?

ANDREW: Brent crude jumped to about a hundred and two dollars a barrel. US crude topped a hundred and four. And that brings us to what the war is already doing to prices here at home. The March consumer price index came out last Thursday, and it was the hottest print in years. Headline inflation surged to three point three percent year over year, up from two point four in February. The Bureau of Labor Statistics said energy prices rose nearly eleven percent in March. Gasoline alone was up twenty-one percent, and that accounted for roughly three quarters of the entire monthly increase.

JENNY: So the war is now showing up directly in consumer prices.

ANDREW: In a big way. And with oil still above a hundred dollars, the April report is unlikely to be much better. That is the backdrop for one of the most consequential Fed leadership transitions in modern history. Jay Powell's term as chair ends May fifteenth. President Trump's nominee, Kevin Warsh, was named back on January thirtieth, but his confirmation has been stalled. The good news is that Bloomberg and Axios both reported today that the Senate Banking Committee has now set Warsh's confirmation hearing for next Monday, April twenty-first.

JENNY: So there is a timeline now.

ANDREW: There is, but there is also a significant obstacle. Senator Thom Tillis of North Carolina says he will not vote for Warsh until the Justice Department drops its investigation into Powell. A Tillis spokesperson softened that slightly, saying the senator will support Warsh once the DOJ investigation concludes, but there is no timeline on that. New financial disclosures filed ahead of the hearing show Warsh's net worth is in the nine figures, which would make him the wealthiest Fed chair on record. Powell has said he will stay on as acting chair if his replacement is not confirmed by May fifteenth.

JENNY: So we could have an acting Fed chair right as inflation is running at three point three percent.

ANDREW: That is the scenario the bond market is watching closely. Jenny, over to you on the home front.

JENNY: Weather-wise, Jacksonville is looking at highs in the low eighties and dry conditions today. And dry is the word to focus on. The city is in the middle of a severe drought with a rainfall deficit in the twenty to twenty-five inch range. Meteorologists are giving us only about a ten percent chance of rain this week, so water conservation remains a concern.

JENNY: Updating a story we have been tracking closely. State Attorney Melissa Nelson's office sent JEA a subpoena on Friday, and the scope is significant. She is asking for emails, texts, and communication records involving CEO Vickie Cavey, City Council President Kevin Carrico, and the lobbying firm Ballard Partners, which employs former Mayor Lenny Curry. The subpoena also asks for records containing specific keywords including racist and toxic.

ANDREW: What exactly is the State Attorney investigating?

JENNY: It connects to a JEA board appointment controversy. Carrico sent a text saying he owed a, quote, big favor to a friend and planned to put that person on the JEA board. That surfaced publicly, and the situation escalated. The City Council formed a special investigative committee that has now met three times. Mayor Donna Deegan has called the allegations against Cavey a smear campaign, arguing this all started because Cavey refused to renew a lobbying contract with Ballard Partners. So the subpoena is trying to sort out whether the pressure on Cavey involves legitimate workplace concerns or political retaliation. Two JEA board positions are currently on hold during the investigation. If you lived through the twenty-nineteen JEA privatization scandal, this dynamic is going to feel familiar.

JENNY: Also in Jacksonville, a twelve million dollar incentive package to keep Winn-Dixie's headquarters in the city cleared the Mayor's Budget Review Committee yesterday. The deal has two parts. A six and a half million dollar headquarters retention grant paid over five years, and five and a half million in property tax rebates over twenty years. In return, Winn-Dixie is committing to a sixty-five million dollar capital investment and two hundred new jobs paying at least a hundred thousand dollars a year at its Edgewood Court offices. That would bring the headquarters workforce to seven hundred.

ANDREW: Two hundred jobs at six figures is meaningful. What is the timeline?

JENNY: The legislation could be introduced to City Council on April twenty-eighth. The concern if this falls through is that Winn-Dixie's parent company could consolidate headquarters operations elsewhere. Jacksonville has been their home base for decades, so both sides have motivation to close this.

JENNY: One more quick local item. The Jacksonville Transportation Authority's fare reduction pilot is producing the opposite of what was intended. The Jax Daily Record reported that ridership on fixed bus routes fell about eighteen percent in February and ten percent in March compared to last year, even after JTA slashed fares from a dollar seventy-five to a dollar. The agency expected cheaper fares would bring more riders. The early data suggests the problem may not be price but network design and service frequency. Andrew, what are you seeing on the mortgage side?

ANDREW: The thirty-year fixed at six point three one percent per Mortgage News Daily is the lowest reading in about four weeks. That reflects some easing tied to ceasefire hopes and the rally in Treasuries. But here is the tension. The March CPI we just discussed, three point three percent headline, is working directly against any sustained rate relief. Lenders price in inflation expectations, and when you see energy-driven spikes like this, the market gets cautious about where rates are headed.

JENNY: So rates came down slightly, but the inflation data could push them right back up.

ANDREW: That is exactly the risk. The ten-year Treasury at four point two nine is holding relatively steady, but if oil stays above a hundred dollars and the April CPI shows continued energy pass-through, mortgage rates could drift back toward the high sixes. For buyers, this narrow window in the low sixes may look good in hindsight. On the regulatory front, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and the Federal Housing Finance Agency were both expected to issue guidance this month on artificial intelligence use in underwriting. That has now slipped to May at the earliest, according to National Mortgage News. For lenders holding back automated decisioning investments waiting for clarity, that is another month in limbo.

JENNY: Speaking of AI, the tech beat had a massive week. Anthropic has officially passed OpenAI in revenue. The company hit thirty billion dollars in annualized revenue, up from about fourteen billion in February. That is more than a doubling in roughly eight weeks. And more than a thousand business customers are now spending over a million dollars a year with Anthropic.

ANDREW: What is driving growth that fast?

JENNY: Claude Code is the biggest single factor. It is generating over two and a half billion in annual revenue on its own and accounts for more than half of all enterprise spending on Anthropic products. But eighty percent of Anthropic's total revenue comes from enterprise API usage, not consumer subscriptions. They have deep traction in financial services, legal, and now healthcare after acquiring a biotech startup called Coefficient Bio earlier this month to push into drug discovery.

JENNY: Meta also made a notable move last week. They launched a new model called Muse Spark, the first from their Superintelligence Labs division under Alexandr Wang. And here is the key detail. It is proprietary. The weights are closed. That is a sharp departure from Meta's open-source Llama strategy that defined them for the past two years.

ANDREW: So Meta is going closed-source.

JENNY: For this model, at least. They said they hope to open-source future versions, but Muse Spark is powering the Meta AI app right now as a closed system. Meta said the model matches their older mid-size Llama for an order of magnitude less compute. It is a signal that even the loudest open-source champion in AI will keep its best work proprietary when the competitive pressure is high enough.

ANDREW: Before we let you go, one thing to watch this week. Kevin Warsh's confirmation hearing next Monday, April twenty-first, before the Senate Banking Committee. This is the first public vetting of the person who could be running the Federal Reserve as soon as mid-May. Inflation is at three point three percent. The Iran war is keeping oil above a hundred dollars. And the nominee's financial disclosures reveal potential conflicts that senators from both parties will want to probe. Watch whether Tillis signals flexibility on his hold, and listen closely for any hints from Warsh on the rate path. The bond market and mortgage rates will move on what he says.

JENNY: That is your Morning Brief for Tuesday. Have a great day.

ANDREW: We will see you tomorrow.
